comments.comment_text:
What does the United States gain from its relationship with Israel?

In general, we all know what Israel gains from its relationship with the U.S. But what does the U.S. gain from Israel?

Let’s look at it from an economic angle.

The common story is simple: the United States is the rich and generous “sugar daddy,” and Israel is the small dependent child living on an allowance of $3.8 billion a year. American taxpayers believe they are paying for our falafel and that we live at their expense. At least that’s what some antisemitic voices on X claim.

And we Israelis? Many of us feel we owe Uncle Sam our lives.

But what if I told you this is actually one of the best financial investments the U.S. government has ever made?

I’ll show you that this is not really “foreign aid.” It is actually a huge subsidy for American industry, a technological shortcut that saves the Pentagon decades, and a money machine that returns hundreds of percent in value.

No politics. No emotions. Just business — through the eyes of American policymakers.

⸻

The numbers
	•	The United States sends $3.8 billion per year to Israel
($3.3 billion in Foreign Military Financing – FMF, and about $500 million for missile defense).
	•	The 2016 Memorandum of Understanding gradually cancels the OSP mechanism (which allowed Israel to convert about 26% of the aid into shekels to buy from Israeli companies).
By 2028, 100% of the aid must be spent in the United States, on American companies.
	•	This aid directly supports more than 20,000 jobs in the United States, and indirectly supports tens of thousands more through about 1,000 subcontractors in 48 states.
	•	Bilateral trade reached about $55 billion in 2024.
Israeli foreign direct investment (FDI) in the U.S. tripled in the last decade to about $24 billion.
Israel is the second-largest source of foreign companies listed on NASDAQ, after China.
	•	Companies founded by Israelis generate $19.5 billion in GDP and employ 57,000 workers in New York alone.
In Florida the number is $7.3 billion, and in Massachusetts Israeli companies are responsible for almost 4% of the state’s GDP.
	•	Return on investment in R&D:
Joint funds such as BIRD generated over $10 billion in revenue.
The agricultural BARD fund returns about $16.50 to the U.S. economy for every $1 invested.

⸻

How the system really works

Stop thinking that the U.S. simply donates money to Israel.
The relationship has become a strategic ecosystem of mutual dependence.

1. A subsidy for American industry

The $3.8 billion the U.S. “gives” Israel leaves the U.S. Treasury, moves to a federal account — and then goes straight back to American defense companies like Lockheed Martin, Boeing, and Raytheon.

Because the conversion to shekels is being phased out, about $800 million that used to enter the Israeli economy now returns to the U.S. economy.

These rules also push Israeli defense companies such as Elbit, Rafael, and Israel Aerospace Industries to establish subsidiaries in the U.S.

Example: Rafael and Raytheon are building a $33 billion facility in Arkansas to produce Iron Dome interceptors.
Israeli technology — American manufacturing — American jobs.

⸻

2. The live battlefield laboratory

This may be the biggest asset the U.S. gets from Israel.

The Pentagon is slow. Developing and deploying a new weapon system takes about 12 years on average.

Israel operates on a stopwatch.

Israel takes American platforms, uses them in high-intensity real combat, and sends the Americans data worth billions.

Take the F-35. Israeli pilots push the aircraft to the edge of its operational limits. The feedback they send back to Lockheed Martin leads to upgrades in software, electronic warfare, and payload capability.

Lockheed Martin’s CEO has said this feedback is “worth billions.”

The U.S. saves decades of testing and hundreds of billions in R&D, because Israel effectively performs real-world combat testing.

When the U.S. Army needed active protection systems for tanks, it bought Israeli systems like Trophy off the shelf instead of spending a decade developing its own.

⸻

3. A pillar of the American technology economy

Beyond defense, Israel is deeply tied to the U.S. tech economy.

About 430 multinational R&D centers operate in Israel, most belonging to American tech giants such as Intel, Microsoft, Google, and Apple.

Major technologies that shaped the PC and cloud era — including Intel processors and early Wi-Fi chips — were developed in Israel.

Israel also accounts for about 20% of global cybersecurity investment.

Israeli cybersecurity firms — many founded by alumni of Unit 8200 — protect Fortune 500 companies, banks, airports, and critical infrastructure in the United States.

In practice, Israel helps maintain American technological dominance against competitors like China.

⸻

What would happen if the aid stopped?

Imagine that tomorrow a U.S. administration cancels the $3.8 billion aid package.

Who loses more?

The immediate losers would be American defense companies that suddenly lose major long-term contracts and orders.

Production lines would slow down. Economies of scale would disappear. The cost per missile, aircraft, or helicopter that the Pentagon buys for itself would increase.

Tens of thousands of American jobs would be affected.

Strategically, the U.S. would also lose its combat laboratory.

Instead of receiving mature, battle-tested technologies, the U.S. military would have to pay 100% of the R&D costs itself, and development timelines would become much longer.

While the U.S. bureaucracy moves slowly, China and Russia would keep advancing.

⸻

Bottom line

This analysis only looks at the economic angle, but the conclusion is clear:

This is not charity.

The “aid” is actually:
	•	an economic stimulus for American industry
	•	a VIP ticket to cutting-edge military technology
	•	an insurance policy for U.S. technological and supply-chain dominance

The debate about “ending American aid” misses the point.

The relationship between the United States and Israel is shifting from donor–recipient to full strategic partnership with mutual economic and security dependence.

The U.S. gets global strategic dominance, jobs in Texas, and cybersecurity protection for Wall Street — all for a tiny fraction of its national budget.

If this were a stock on the market, Warren Buffett would probably buy all of it.
